coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence enantioselective sensing of lactic acid enantiomers by Zn-MOC@CQDs with double chiral building blocks

coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Xinwen
Lu, Mengyun
Zhang, Wenjing
Zhao, Wuduo
Yu, Ajuan
Zhang, Shusheng
coredata.dc:description: Chiral carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with –SH, –NH2 functional groups were grafted onto a metal–organic cage (Zn-MOC) with unsaturated metal sites through coordination bond, and then a novel bichiral composite Zn-MOC@CQDs was constructed for the chiral discrimination of LA enantiomers. Zn-MOC@CQDs exhibited a high fluorescence detection sensitivity for L-LA instead of D-LA, and the enantioselectivity coefficient for LA enantiomers was 2.34. Zn-MOC@CQDs exhibited more excellent enantioselectivity than the single components of CQDs and Zn-MOC, and the key reason may be the chiral synergistic effect between the different chiral building blocks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic cage
Carbon dot
Bichiral composite
lactic acid (LA)
Fluorescence sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis of N, S co-doped CQDs
1H NMR titration experiment measurement of D- or L-LA to Zn-MOC@CQDs
The feasibility of enantiomeric sensing of LA with Zn-MOC@CQDs
Optimization of LA enantiomers sensing conditions
Detection of L-lactate in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (a) CQDs; SEM images of (b) Zn-MOC; (c) 4–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs; (d) 2–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs; (e) 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs; (f) 1–3 Zn-MOC@CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of CQDs, Zn-MOC and Zn-MOC@CQDs; (b) PXRD patterns of Zn-MOC and Zn-MOC@CQDs; (c) N 1s XPS spectra of Zn-MOC and Zn-MOC@CQDs; (d) S 2p XPS spectra of CQDs and Zn-MOC@CQDs.

(a) The effect of D-LA concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of 10 ppm of 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs; (b) The effect of L-LA concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of 10 ppm of 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs; (c) The relationship curve between I512/I457 value of 1-1 Zn-MOC@CQDs and the concentrations of D-LA; (d) The The relationship curve between I512/I457 value of 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs and the concentrations of L-LA.

(a and b) 1H NMR titration spectra of D-LA to 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs; (c and d) 1H NMR titration spectra of L-LA to 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs. Where lines a, f represented Zn-MOC@CQDs and D/L-LA, respectively; Lines b-e represented the mixtures of Zn-MOC@CQDs and D/L-DA with the equivalence ratios of 1:1, 1:2, 1:3 and 1:4, respectively.
The fabrication of bichiral fluorescence sensor Zn-MOC@CQDs and the recognition to LA enantiomers.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence enantioselective sensing of lactic acid enantiomers by Zn-MOC@CQDs with double chiral building blocks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Molecular Sensing and Harmful Substances Detection Technology, Zhengzhou University, Kexue Avenue 100, Zhengzhou, Henan 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Molecular Sensing and Harmful Substances Detection Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Molecular Sensing and Harmful Substances Detection Technology, Zhengzhou University, Kexue Avenue 100, Zhengzhou, Henan 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Advanced Analysis and Gene Sequencing, Key Laboratory of Molecular Sensing and Harmful Substances Detection Technology, Zhengzhou University, Kexue Avenue 100, Zhengzhou, Henan 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Advanced Analysis and Gene Sequencing
Key Laboratory of Molecular Sensing and Harmful Substances Detection Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Advanced Analysis and Gene Sequencing, Key Laboratory of Molecular Sensing and Harmful Substances Detection Technology, Zhengzhou University, Kexue Avenue 100, Zhengzhou, Henan 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chiral carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with –SH, –NH2 functional groups were grafted onto a metal–organic cage (Zn-MOC) with unsaturated metal sites through coordination bond, and then a novel bichiral composite Zn-MOC@CQDs was constructed for the chiral discrimination of LA enantiomers. Zn-MOC@CQDs exhibited a high fluorescence detection sensitivity for L-LA instead of D-LA, and the enantioselectivity coefficient for LA enantiomers was 2.34. Zn-MOC@CQDs exhibited more excellent enantioselectivity than the single components of CQDs and Zn-MOC, and the key reason may be the chiral synergistic effect between the different chiral building blocks.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lactic acid (LA) is a type of α-hydroxyl carboxylic acid with optical activity. Both L-LA and D-LA have important biological significance in living systems [1,2]. For example, L-LA was a sensitive indicator of tissue hypoxia, while D-LA is closely related to many diseases such as cancer [3], diabetes [4], appendicitis [5], acidosis [6], etc. The detection methods associated with LA enantiomers include the capillary electrophoresis (CE) [7], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry with chiral derivatization (GC–MS) [8], nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) [9], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,11], chemical sensors [12,13], etc. In recent years, the chiral metal–organic frameworks [14] and photonic crystal fiber-based fluorescence sensors [15] have been developed and applied for the quantitative detection of LA enantiomers.

Metal-organic cages (MOCs) are a class of discrete nanocages built from metal centers and flexible ligands through coordination-driven self-assembly [16], which have aroused the considerable attention due to their fascinating structures such as specific cavities, nanoscale sizes, well-defined shapes and easy to functionalize, etc. [17–19]. The solubility of MOCs in common solvents could be regulated by functional modification, and their excellent solution processability provides the possibility of serving as molecular building blocks to fabricate composite [20–22]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a category of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with remarkable optical properties and good biocompatibility, which can form functional composite with MOFs [23–25], Au nanoparticles [26], polymer films [27] and other building blocks. So far, however, there are no report on the dual chiral fluorescence sensor composed of CQDs and chiral metal–organic cages (CMOCs).

The chiral recognition performance of chiral functional porous materials is closely related to the species and quantities of chiral recognition sites. However, most reported chiral materials only have a single chiral building block [28]. Compared with single chiral building block, the emergence of bichiral composite represents an effective solution to address the challenges of low recognition ability and limited separation scope in chiral separation [29–31]. Gu et al. [32] prepared a bichiral building block based enantiopure CPM films ((R, Δ)-CPM and (S, Λ)-CPM) containing MOCs and metal complexes through a layer-by-layer growth strategy, which exhibited superior chiral selectivity performance for the enantiomers of 2-butanol and methyl-lactate. Professor Hou [33] established an electrochemical chiral sensor with two kinds of chiral sites (chiral cavity of β-cyclodextrin and chiral backbone of Ca-sacc/MeOH) by electrooxidizing β-cyclodextrin onto Ca-sacc/MeOH. The proposed multichiral β-CD@Ca-sacc/MeOH/GCE could be used for recognition of tryptophan and penicillamine enantiomers simultaneously with wide linear range, low detection limits value, high repeatability and stability within the available electrochemical window. Although the bichiral building block composite has great potential in the field of chiral recognition, the issue at hand is how to design reasonably the structure then further improve effectively the enantiomer separation capabilities of the bichiral building block [34] still faces enormous challenges.

Here, a novel bichiral fluorescence sensor Zn-MOC@CQDs was constructed by grafting chiral carbon dots onto a soluble chiral metal–organic cage (Zn-MOC). A thorough characterization of the morphology and crystal structure for this composite was carried out with scanning electron microscopy (SEM), infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS). The chiral recognition performance of fluorescent sensor Zn-MOC@CQDs was assessed by fluorescence spectroscopy, and a comparison of the enantioselectivity performances among MOC@CQDs and the single chiral building blocks (CQDs and Zn-MOC) was also performed. Furthermore, the synergistic mechanism of chiral recognition sites was investigated by 1H NMR spectroscopy (Scheme 1 ).

L-cysteine (98.5 %) was purchased from Bailun Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). (1R,2R)-(−)-1,2-diaminocyclohexane (97.0 %) and 4-tert-butyl-2,6-diformylphenol (95.0 %) were obtained from Haohong Biopharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Zinc acetate dihydrate (AR) was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid (99.5 %), D-lactic acid (D-LA) and L-lactic acid (L-LA) were achieved from Aladdin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloroform (CHCl3) was got from Luoyang Chemical Reagent Factory (Henan, China). Methanol (CH3OH), acetonitrile (CH3CN) and ethanol (CH3CH2OH) were gained from Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed on Zeiss Auriga-bu SEM (Germany). Transmission electron microscope was carried out on Talos L120CG2 120 KV TEM (Czech Republic). Infrared spectra were recorded on an IR Tracer-100 spectrometer (American). PXRD patterns were conducted on Empyrean powder X-ray diffractometer (Netherlands). Liquid nuclear magnetic resonance hydrogen spectrum was detected on Avance Ⅱ 400MH 1H NMR spectrometer (China). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on TU-1901 dual-beam ultraviolet/visible spectrophotometer (China). Fluorescence spectra were executed on FL-6000 spectrophotometer (Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was proceeded on AXIS Supra XPS (Japan). The water was taken from the Milli-Q instrument (American).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of N, S co-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: N, S co-doped CQDs was prepared according to the previous method [35] as follows: 1.0 g of citric acid and 0.25 g of L-cysteine were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water and then treated hydrothermally in a PTFE high-pressure reactor (100 mL) at 180 ℃ for 8 h. The obtained yellow solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and filtered with the 0.22 μm PES membrane at least twice, then dialyzed for 12 h in deionized water. The CQDs was obtained after the step of freeze drying.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The preparation of [3 + 3] macrocycle ligand L 1

ce:section.ce:para:
L 1 was prepared according to the reported method [36]. 2.062 g of 4-tert-buty-l-2,6-diformylphenol was dissolved in 20 mL of CH3CN, and 1.142 g of (1R, 2R)–diaminocyclohexane was dissolved in 15 mL of CH3CN. After mixed, the yellow suspension was stirred at 50 ℃ for 12 h, filtered out, washed with CH3CN and dried to yield the yellow powder.
(2)
The synthesis of Zn-MOC (Zn3 L 2)

Metal-organic cage Zn3 L 2 was prepared as follows[36]: 0.1045 g of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O and 0.2850 g of L 1 were added in 50 mL of CH3OH. The mixture was placed at room temperature for 2 h and stood in the fridge overnight at 4 ℃, then the light yellow product Zn3 L 2 was recrystallized from chloroform and dried under vacuum. Before reacted with CQDs, Zn-MOC must be purified in CH3OH through Soxhlet extraction.

20.0 mg of Zn-MOC was dissolved in 2.5 mL of CHCl3, and 20.0 mg of CQDs was dispersed in 5 mL of CH3CH2OH solution. After the solution of CQDs was added to the solution of Zn-MOC, the mixture was stirred for 20 min, then it was observed that the solution gradually changed from clear to turbid. After reacted for 24 h, the mixture was centrifuged, alternately washed with CHCl3 and CH3CH2OH then dried to obtain the orange power

The volume ratio of CHCl3 and CH3CH2OH remained the same as 1:2 in the synthetic process. The dosages of CQDs were 5.0 mg, 10.0 mg, 60.0 mg in turn, and the obtained products were named as 4–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs, 2–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs, 1–3 Zn-MOC@CQDs, respectively.

The CHCl3 solution of Zn-MOC@CQDs with a concentration of 10 ppm was prepared and preserved as the probe stock solution. 10.0 g/L of D- or L-LA was regarded as the mother liquor, and the different concentrations included 0.3 g/L, 0.6 g/L, 0.9 g/L, 1.2 g/L, 1.8 g/L, 3.0 g/L, 4.2 g/L of the D- or L-LA standard solutions were prepared by diluting stepwise. Then 50 μL of D- or L-LA standard solutions with different concentrations were added into 3.0 mL of probe solution, and the concentrations of the D-/L-LA were 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 50, 70 ppm, respectively. The fluorescence spectroscopy was measured after shaken at room temperature for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 1H NMR titration experiment measurement of D- or L-LA to Zn-MOC@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 2.5 mg of Zn-MOC@CQDs was uniformly dispersed in 0.5 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 solution, and the initial 1H NMR spectrum was measured. The solution of D-LA with a concentration of 0.15 mol/L was prepared for later use. Four copies of D-LA solution 6 μL were added to the probe solution for four consecutive times, and the mixtures were stood at room temperature for 1 h after each drop, then the 1H NMR spectra were conducted. Similarly, the 1H NMR titration process of L-LA to Zn-MOC@CQDs was the same as that of D-LA.

Sweat was obtained according to the reported method [37], and then L-LA sample solutions of different concentrations were prepared using the standard addition method. The lactate concentrations in sweat were also detected by the Human L-lactic acid Elisa kits, and quantitative results obtained with both methods were contrasted. Furthermore, fluorescence sensing experiments were performed to determine the recovery.

The morphologies of CQDs, Zn-MOC and Zn-MOC@CQDs were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, chiral CQDs exhibited good dispersibility with particle sizes approximately below 5 nm. A Soxhlet extraction operation was needed for Zn-MOC to remove unreacted impurity molecules before bonded with CQDs, and the shape of the purified Zn-MOC crystal was fusiform with sizes of 2 μm (Fig. 1b). Due to the high porosity and excellent solution processability, Zn-MOC could be served as a molecular container to construct the composite with CQDs through post-synthesis modification strategies. It could be seen that the feed ratio of CQDs and Zn-MOC had a significant impact on the overall morphology of the bichiral MOC@CQDs composite in Fig. 1c–f. When the mass ratio of CQDs and Zn-MOC was 1:4, the crystal of obtained composite was rod-shape with sizes of about 8 μm (Fig. 1c). As the ratio increased to 1:2, the flower-like crystals consisted of short-rods with sizes of about 500 nm were obtained (Fig. 1d). While the proportion continued to ascend to 1:1 and 3:1, the spherical and hemispherical crystals with sizes of approximately 200–300 nm appeared, respectively (Fig. 1e and f). With the increase of feeding amount of hydrophilic CQDs, more CQDs were encapsulated in the hydrophobic cavity through the coordinate interaction with Zn2+, which influenced the surface chemical environment of bichiral composite, resulting in the change of the microscopic morphology of Zn-MOC@CQDs crystals.

FT-IR and PXRD characterizations were performed and the results were displayed in Fig. 2 and Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. 2a, in the IR spectrum of CQDs, the absorption peaks at 3420 cm−1, 3117 cm−1, 2594 cm−1 and 1732 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH, NH, SH, and CO, respectively. The peak in the spectrum of Zn-MOC at 1622 cm−1 belonged to the CC or CN stretching vibration on the benzene ring, and the peak at 2950 cm−1 owned to the CH stretching vibration from the tert-butyl group. It was observed that the characteristic absorption peaks of Zn-MOC and the absorption peak at 1720 cm−1 of CQDs simultaneously appeared in the spectrum of Zn-MOC@CQDs, indicating the successful preparation of bichiral composite. The IR absorption peaks of NH and SH groups from CQDs disappeared after reacted with Zn-MOC, which confirmed the coordinate interaction between these two building blocks. The diffraction peaks of Zn-MOC@CQDs with 2θ at 6.3°, 6.9°, 11.8° and 12.9° were assigned to the (1 0 1), (0 0 3), (2 0 1), and (1 0 5) crystal planes, respectively, corresponding to Zn-MOC in the nanocomposite. Compared with Zn-MOC, the diffraction peak at 6.3° was not observed in the PXRD spectrum of Zn-MOC@CQDs, suggesting the change of crystal chemical structure caused by the formation of coordinate bond between CQDs and Zn-MOC (Fig. 2b). In addition, as shown in Fig. S1, the IR and PXRD spectra remained almost unchanged with the increased feeding ratio of CQDs and Zn-MOC from 1:4 to 3:1, indicating that the crystal structure of bichiral composite was not influenced by the content of CQDs.

XPS spectra were performed to further understand the elemental composition of the obtained Zn-MOC@CQDs. As depicted in Fig. S2, XPS full spectra of Zn-MOC@CQDs presented C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Zn 2p and S 2p signal peaks due to the load of CQDs to Zn-MOC. In the N 1s spectra of Zn-MOC shown in Fig. 2c, the signal peaks at 398.5 eV and 401.9 eV were assigned to the CNC and Zn-N bonds, respectively. Compared with Zn-MOC, the relative content of N-Zn bond increased in the N 1s XPS spectrum. Meanwhile, the binding energy (BE) of CNC bond exhibited an increase of about 0.3 eV and the BE of Zn-N bond showed a decrease of about 0.7 eV, which demonstrated the coordinate interaction between NH2 group of CQDs and the unsaturated Zn sites of Zn-MOC. From the high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum of CQDs in Fig. 2d, the BE at 163.4 eV and 164.6 eV corresponded to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2. Sulfur element signal peaks appeared in the XPS spectrum of Zn-MOC@CQDs, and both the BE of S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 decreased 0.2 eV, confirming the bonding between SH group of CQDs and unsaturated Zn sites of Zn-MOC. Therefore, the construction of bichiral composite was based on the coordinate interaction between CQDs and Zn-MOC.

Furthermore, the 1H NMR measurement were performed to verify the fabrication of Zn-MOC@CQDs. Fig. S3-6 displayed the 1H NMR spectra of L1 , Zn-MOC, CQDs and Zn-MOC@CQDs, respectively. The 1H NMR spectra of L1 and Zn-MOC were consistent with the present report[36], which indicated the successful synthesis of Zn-MOC with high purity. In the 1H NMR spectrum of Zn-MOC@CQDs, the absorption peaks at 2.60–2.80 ppm confirmed the presence of chiral CQDs, as well as the emergence of a deviation in the characteristic chemical shift of Zn-MOC absorption peaks in comparison with the initial purified product, indicating the combination of two chiral building blocks. Circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy was conducted to confirm the chirality of Zn-MOC@CQDs. In the spectrum of Zn-MOC@CQDs (Fig. S7), the peaks at 240 nm and 270 nm were assigned to MOC chiral ligands [38], while the absorption peaks of 250 nm and 350 nm were corresponding to CQDs [39]. It was shown that Zn-MOC@CQDs simultaneously retained both the chiral centers of Zn-MOC and CQDs. The Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) surface areas measurement were further performed, which were calculated to be around 53 m2 g−1 with pores sizes of 4 nm for Zn-MOC@CQDs (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of enantiomeric sensing of LA with Zn-MOC@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical performance of Zn-MOC and Zn-MOC@CQDs was analyzed by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. S9, the ligand L1 exhibited two absorption peaks at 346 nm and 437 nm. The Zn-MOC showed an absorption peak at 396 nm and a strong blue fluorescence emission peak at 462 nm, which may be assigned to the n-π* and π-π* electronic transitions. In the spectrum of Zn-MOC@CQDs, although the peak associated with CQDs was not appeared at about 350 nm, there was a red-shift for the peak at 403 nm and a new peak presented at 458 nm. Besides, this bichiral compositel exhibited two fluorescence emission peaks at 457 nm and 512 nm with a significantly decreased fluorescence intensity compared with Zn-MOC. This phenomenon may be due to the coordinate interaction between CQDs and Zn-MOC, which affected the metal-to-ligand charge transfer and further suppressed the light absorption of the composite. The quantum yields (QY) of the prepared CQDs and Zn-MOC@CQDs were measured at λex = 360 nm in 0.1 M H2SO4 using quinine sulfate (QS) as a reference with the values of 54 % and 32 %, respectively. The values of the CQDs were in general agreement with those reported in the literature.[40].

Bichiral composite Zn-MOC@CQDs had rich chiral centers, unique steric configuration and excellent optical performance, which could be regarded as a proportional fluorescence sensor for enantiomeric recognition. 0.167 mM of D-LA or L-LA solution was added to the 10 ppm of 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs probe solution, and the fluorescence spectroscopy was investigated after shaken at room temperature for 10 min. The results in Fig.S10 showed that the peak intensities of the probe decreased at 457 nm but increased at 512 nm after treated with L- or D-LA. There was a greater change in the intensity ratio of two fluorescence emission peaks (I512/I457) caused by L-LA, indicating that Zn-MOC@CQDs exhibited the fluorescence enantioselective recognition for LA isomers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of LA enantiomers sensing conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
Taking the response time as the abscissa and I512/I457 as the ordinate, the response time curves of 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs to L- or D-LA were constructed. As shown in Fig. S11, the I512/I457 value gradually increased at first and maintained constant 5 min later, as well as the increment of L-LA being significantly greater than that of D-LA. The enantiomeric sensing performance of the bichiral Zn-MOC@CQDs fluorescent sensor for L-/D-LA was investigated by varying the feeding ratio of CQDs and Zn-MOC. The enantioselectivity coefficient (α) was regarded as an indicator of enantiomeric recognition performance. Among them, α = K L
-LA/K D
-LA, K L
-LA and K D
-LA were the slopes of the relationship curves between L-LA or D-LA concentrations and I512/I457 values, respectively. It could be seen in Fig. 3 , Figs. S12–14 and Table S1 that the α value increased from 1.33 to 2.34 with the increase of the feeding ratio of CQDs and Zn-MOC from 1:4 to 1:1. The better and better enantioselectivity of Zn-MOC@CQDs may be due in large part to the adequate exposure of effective chiral active sites caused by the decreased crystal sizes from micrometers to nanoscale as well as the synergistic effect from different chiral centers. However, when the proportion of CQDs further increased, the porosity of bichiral composite was affected, which greatly inhibited the chiral discrimination of LA enantiomers. Therefore, the optimal ratio of CQDs and Zn-MOC was 1:1.

To improve the sensing performance of 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs, the concentration of the probe was further optimized. As shown in Fig. 3, Figs. S15, S16 and Table S2, with the increase of the probe concentration, the α value first ascended and then decreased. A satisfactory chiral recognition performance of 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs was achieved with a concentration of 10 ppm. Under the optimal conditions, the standard curve equation for D-LA was y = 13.64x + 1.54 with R2 = 0.994, while the standard curve equation for L-LA was y = 31.85x + 0.35 with R2 = 0.990, and the calculated α value was 2.34 (Fig. 3c and d).

In addition, 0.55 mM of D-/L-LA mixed solution with the different enantiomeric excess (ee) was prepared, and then 50 μL D-/L-LA solution was added into the 10 ppm of 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs probe solution. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra were investigated under optimal conditions and the relationship curve between the I512/I457 value and ee value was shown in Fig. S17. In this experiment, ee = (L − D)/(L + D), L and D were referred to the content of L-LA and D-LA, respectively. It was observed that the I512/I457 value of 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs increased exponentially with the increment proportion of L-LA in the D-/L-LA mixed solution, further proving that the probe had a higher fluorescence response sensitivity to L-LA isomer.

To verify that the introduction of the second type of chiral center can improve the chiral recognition ability of composite, control experiments of Zn-MOC and CQDs fluorescent sensing on LA enantiomers were conducted, respectively. As shown in Fig. S18, the fluorescence intensities of the CQDs were nearly unchanged after added L-LA or D-LA with a concentration ranging from 0 to 0.77 mM, indicating that CQDs could not distinguish two configurations of LA. Furthermore, LA enantiomers with the same concentration range were added to the 10 ppm of Zn-MOC probe solution, and the fluorescence spectra were determined after sufficient reaction (Fig. S19). The emission peak intensity at 460 nm gradually decreased with increased content of D-LA or L-LA. When the concentration of LA in the reaction system exceeded 0.11 mM, a new fluorescence emission peak appeared at 512 nm, and the peak intensity was positively correlated with the analyte concentration. The linear regression equation between the concentration of D-LA and I512/I460 value was y = 36.52x − 0.09 with R2 = 0.997, while the linear regression equation between the concentration of L-LA and I512/I460 value was y = 44.02x − 0.03 with R2 = 0.994. The calculated α value was only 1.21, suggesting a poor chiral recognition ability of chiral Zn-MOC for LA enantiomers.

From the above results it could be concluded that bichiral Zn-MOC@CQDs exhibited higher enantioselectivity in comparison with CQDs and Zn-MOCs. To further explain the reason, nuclear magnetic titration experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. 4 a and c, the absorption peaks at 2.56–2.75 ppm were assigned to the chiral proton hydrogen of CQDs encapsulated in the MOC cages. With the increment of D-LA, the chemical shifts at 2.58 ppm, 2.62 ppm, 2.68 ppm and 2.71 ppm shifted by 0.01 ppm, 0.02 ppm, 0.01 ppm, 0.01 ppm towards the high magnetic field, respectively, while a larger shifts of 0.03 ppm, 0.04 ppm, 0.04 ppm emerged for the the peaks at 2.62 ppm, 2.68 ppm and 2.71 ppm along with the increase of L-LA contents. Besides, the chemical shift peaks at 1.02–1.11 ppm in Fig. 4b and d were attributed to the chiral CH bond on the skeleton of Zn-MOC in bichiral composite. It could be seen more clearly that the influence of D-LA on the absorption peak at 1.098 ppm was negligible, while L-LA yielded a larger shift on this peak. In contrast with D-LA, the greater shift changes caused by L-LA may be due to the more matched stereo configuration and the stronger host–guest interactions including hydrogen bonding interaction and other weak intermolecular forces between L-LA and the bichiral Zn-MOC@CQDs.

Moreover, the improvement in the enantiomer recognition performance of bichiral Zn-MOC@CQDs could be attributed to the chiral synergistic effects from two kinds of chiral centers. On the one hand, the NH2 and SH functional groups of chiral CQDs coordinated with the metal ions of chiral Zn-MOC, thus a unique chiral microenvironment formed by the wonderful combination of the two chiral building blocks. On the other hand, control experiments had demonstrated that CQDs played an important role in the recognition of LA enantiomers after loaded onto the metal–organic cages. Therefore, an important way to improve chiral recognition performance was the introduction of the second chiral site into the conventional chiral building block.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of L-lactate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to validate the accuracy and practical applicability of the developed detection methodology, a series of tests were performed to detect the amount of L-LA present in human sweat. As shown in the Table S3, the concentrations of L-LA in sweat ranged from 5 to 20 mM, which was in good agreement with the previous report.[41] Recovery tests of standard addition in human sweats showed the spiked recoveries of 90 %–108 % with RSDs of 0.85 %–3.5 % (n = 3). These results were quite in line with those determined with the commercial Elisa kits, as detailed in Table S4. These results indicated that the developed fluorescence detection method based on this dual chiral fluorescent sensor possessed high precision and was promising for real sample analysis.

In summary, a bichiral composite Zn-MOC@CQDs was fabricated for the chiral fluorimetric recognition of LA enantiomers. The concentration of bichiral composite and the feeding ratio of two chiral building blocks during the preparation process had a significant impact on the chiral recognition performance for LA enantiomers. Under the optimal conditions, 10 ppm of 1–1 Zn-MOC@CQDs exhibited excellent enantioselectivity for LA isomers with an enantioselectivity coefficient of 2.34, which was far superior to the single components of CQDs and Zn-MOC. Chiral synergistic effect between two types of chiral centers was the key reason for the effective improvement of the recognition ability for the LA enantiomers. Compared with traditional methods, fluorescence spectrometry has the advantages of fast response, low cost, simple operation and high sensitivity, and can rapidly detect and separate lactic acid enantiomers. This work provided a promising strategy for the preparation of dual chiral functional building block fluorescent composite and a feasible method for the improved chiral recognition performance.

Xinwen Jia: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Mengyun Lu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Wenjing Zhang: Validation. Wuduo Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Ajuan Yu: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Shusheng Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22474129, 22036003, and 21675144).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112575.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
